title: Building a culture of production ownership
excerpt: Why engineering teams in FinTech should own the pager — and how to make that healthy, sustainable and a growth lever.
content:
## Why Ownership Matters

In modern software engineering, especially in regulated FinTech, the engineers who build a system should also take ownership of running it in production. Since developers understand the application's architecture, business logic, and recent code changes better than anyone else, they can identify and resolve production issues much faster. This creates a shorter feedback loop between detecting a problem, fixing it, and improving the system. In contrast, relying entirely on a separate operations team often delays resolution because the issue has to pass through multiple teams before reaching the developers.

## How We Made It Healthy

### 1. Clear Ownership Boundaries

Each engineering team is responsible only for the services they develop and maintain. Engineers receive production alerts only for their own applications, APIs, databases, queues, and infrastructure. This prevents unnecessary interruptions and ensures accountability. Legacy systems are assigned dedicated owners instead of becoming the responsibility of every engineer.

### 2. Service Level Objectives (SLOs)

Every critical service is assigned measurable reliability targets, such as API availability, response time, and error rate. Alerts are generated only when these targets are breached instead of for every minor event. This significantly reduces alert fatigue and allows engineers to focus only on incidents that impact customers.

Typical SLOs include:

- API Availability: 99.95%
- P95 Response Time: < 300 ms
- Error Rate: < 1%
- Webhook Delivery Success: > 99%

### 3. Fair On-Call Compensation

Production support requires engineers to be available outside normal working hours. To recognize this responsibility, organizations compensate on-call engineers through additional leave, monetary allowance, compensatory off, or flexible working hours. This creates a healthier on-call culture and reduces burnout.

### 4. Blameless Incident Reviews

Every production incident concludes with a Root Cause Analysis (RCA). The purpose is to understand what happened, why it happened, how it was resolved, and how similar incidents can be prevented in the future. The focus is on improving systems, monitoring, testing, and deployment processes—not blaming individuals. Incident reviews are never used during performance evaluations, encouraging engineers to report issues openly and learn from failures.

title: Designing Partner Integrations that Don't Break at 11 PM
excerpt: A practical checklist for resilient, observable third-party integrations in regulated environments.
content:
## Production Readiness Checklist

### 1. Idempotency Keys on Every Write

All APIs performing write operations—such as loan creation, mandate registration, payment initiation, loan disbursal, or document upload—should include an Idempotency Key. This unique identifier ensures that repeated requests caused by retries, network failures, or user actions are processed only once by the partner.

Implementing idempotency prevents duplicate transactions, duplicate loan creation, and inconsistent financial records, making it an essential requirement for any financial system.

### 2. Timeouts at Every Layer

Every external API call should have clearly defined timeout configurations instead of relying on default values.

Recommended timeout categories include:

- Connection Timeout
- Read Timeout
- Write Timeout
- Total Request Timeout

Proper timeout management prevents slow or unresponsive partner systems from blocking application threads, exhausting connection pools, increasing response times, and impacting other services. Fast failure enables the application to retry, fallback, or switch to alternate partners without affecting customer experience.

### 3. Circuit Breakers

Circuit Breakers protect the application from repeatedly calling unhealthy partner APIs.

When failures exceed a configured threshold, the circuit opens and temporarily stops sending requests to the affected partner. After a cooldown period, a limited number of requests are allowed to verify recovery. If the partner has recovered, the circuit closes automatically; otherwise, it remains open.

Circuit Breakers prevent cascading failures, reduce unnecessary traffic to failing systems, and maintain overall platform stability.

### 4. Structured Logging with Correlation IDs

Every customer journey should be traceable across multiple services using a unique Correlation ID.

Each log entry should capture relevant contextual information such as:

- Correlation ID
- Customer ID
- Loan ID
- Partner Name
- API Name
- Request Time
- Response Time
- HTTP Status Code
- Error Code
- Retry Count

Structured logging enables engineers to trace an entire customer journey across microservices, partner APIs, databases, and asynchronous systems without manually correlating unrelated log entries.

### 5. Per-Partner Dashboards

Each lending partner should have a dedicated monitoring dashboard providing complete visibility into API performance and health.

Typical metrics include:

- Total Requests
- Successful Requests
- Failed Requests
- Rejected Requests
- Retry Count
- Timeout Count
- Average Response Time
- P95 and P99 Latency
- Error Rate
- API Availability
- SLA Compliance
- Top Error Codes
- Top Failing APIs

These dashboards help engineering, operations, product, and business teams quickly identify issues affecting a specific partner.

### 6. Synthetic Monitoring

Synthetic Monitoring proactively checks partner availability by sending automated test requests at regular intervals, even when there is no customer traffic.

Each synthetic request validates:

- API Availability
- Authentication
- Response Time
- Response Format
- Business Validations

If a synthetic request fails, an alert is generated before customers begin experiencing failures. This enables engineering teams to respond proactively instead of reactively.

title: From IC to Engineering Manager: what actually changed
excerpt: Notes on the transition — what got harder, what got easier, and the mental models that helped.
content:
## What got harder

- **The work is asynchronous.** As an IC, a good day ends with a green CI build. As a manager, you sometimes can't tell if today's conversation will pay off in three months or never.
- **The leverage is indirect.** Your impact is in other people's PRs, not yours.
- **Feedback loops are slower.** A misjudged hire shows up six months later.

## What got easier

- **Saying no.** Once you're responsible for the team's roadmap, prioritisation stops being a debate and becomes a duty.
- **Asking dumb questions.** Nobody expects the manager to know every line of code. That permission is freeing.

## Mental models that helped

1. **Your job is to make the team better than the sum of its parts.** Everything else is downstream of that.
2. **Hire slowly, performance-manage fast.** The cost of a bad hire compounds; the cost of a hard conversation does not.
3. **The work isn't fungible — but most of it is delegate-able.** Default to delegation; intervene only when something is truly load-bearing.
